answers.answer: Yes. The current backend still makes ProbabilityEdit representative because limiter enforcement and quota-header emission remain centralized for the full protected POST surface. `WRITE_ROUTE_POLICIES` and the protected suffix sets still define one shared five-route write surface, `resolve_write_route_category(...)` still classifies those routes before dispatch, `_enforce_write_controls()` still applies `resolve_write_request_agent(...)` plus `enforce_rate_limit(...)` before `route_request(...)`, and `BayesHandler.handle_api()` still emits quota headers only after successful protected writes or on `rate_limit_exceeded` with `Retry-After`. None of the route handlers own separate limiter logic, so the seam that drives remaining-quota decrement, `429`, `Retry-After`, and per-agent isolation is still shared.
answers.uncertainty: The only way this conclusion would change is if a protected POST route stopped going through the shared classifier and handler adapter, and there is no sign of that in this checkout.

answers.answer: Yes. The current tests already freeze the limiter behavior at the right layer: ProbabilityEdit owns the full matrix for remaining-quota decrement, disabled-limiter header absence, anonymous no-header success, `429 rate_limit_exceeded`, `Retry-After`, and per-agent isolation, while the other protected POST routes are covered by first-success breadth checks rather than cloned exhaustion matrices. The dedicated comment-post success contract and the five-route first-success loop already prove that create-market, comment-post, market-resolve, and event-trade receive enabled-limiter success headers through the same shared seam. Duplicating the full ProbabilityEdit limiter matrix on those routes would therefore repeat the same central contract instead of adding new coverage value.
answers.uncertainty: A broader matrix on another route would still be possible as a policy choice, but the current plan and test inventory do not require it.

answers.answer: No. There is no codebase evidence that the step-3 breadth work introduced a disagreement that would justify cloning the ProbabilityEdit limiter matrix onto another route in this phase. The route-breadth tests added success-path header coverage across all five protected POST routes, but the route handlers themselves still only return payload and status and do not emit rate-limit headers or perform route-local quota accounting. I also reran `python3 tests/test_bayes_market_api.py BayesMarketApiAuthRateLimitTests` in this checkout, and the focused sentinel slice still passed cleanly with 21 tests. In this state, step 4 should remain preservation-only: keep ProbabilityEdit as the sole limiter matrix and only widen cross-route assertions if a future shared-seam failure appears.
answers.uncertainty: The passing focused test run is runtime verification rather than file evidence, so the file citations here anchor the structural reason that no route-specific divergence is expected.

assumption_checks.impact: All five protected POST routes still share the same pre-routing agent-validation and rate-limit seam, so ProbabilityEdit remains representative for limiter behavior.
The existing ProbabilityEdit tests still freeze decrement, `429`, `Retry-After`, and per-agent isolation, while the breadth loop already covers success-path headers on the other protected routes.
There is still no route-local quota or header logic to justify cross-route duplication; any real bug would have to come from the shared write-control seam.
No child task is needed because the relevant plan context, backend code, and focused tests are already enough to answer this step with high confidence.
